title: A British Police Force Has Been Using a Flawed AI to Predict Future Crimes
gist: An investigative report by WIRED, Liberty Investigates, Bristol Cable, and Lighthouse Reports reveals that Avon and Somerset Police in the UK have operated predictive policing AI models with demonstrated precision below 10% for years, while a database covering 500,000 people was used to build models that independent reviewers found 'not fit for operational use.' The UK government simultaneously launched a £75 million national police AI body, raising systemic governance questions about public-sector AI deployment.

themes.theme: Predictive Policing AI Failure and Governance Risk

insights.claim: Avon and Somerset Police's AI burglary prediction model maintained precision below 10% for at least three years of operational use, meaning more than 90% of its predictions were incorrect.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: A sub-10% precision rate in an operational policing context represents systematic misallocation of law enforcement resources and potential civil liberties violations at scale—a benchmark for AI accountability risk in public-sector deployments.
insights.quote: The burglary prediction model had a precision rate of less than 10 percent for at least three years

insights.claim: Avon and Somerset Police's Think Family Database has been aggregating data on approximately 500,000 people in the Bristol area since 2016, providing the training data for 23 separate machine learning models.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: A database of 500,000 people underpinning 23 ML models with no proven operational accuracy demonstrates the scale of unvalidated AI deployment risk in UK public services.

insights.claim: Models built to predict child sexual exploitation (CSE) and child criminal exploitation (CCE) were abandoned after an independent review by Social Finance found them 'not fit for operational use,' with source code unavailable for audit.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Source code unavailability for public-sector AI models is a governance red flag that regulators and auditors are increasingly scrutinizing; this case establishes a precedent for AI procurement and transparency requirements.

insights.claim: The College of Policing's CEO Andy Marsh advocated for police forces to adopt AI 'like heroin,' describing the technology as something forces should inject into operations rapidly—a framing that alarmed civil liberties groups.
insights.speaker: Andy Marsh
insights.so_what: Senior regulatory language normalizing aggressive AI adoption without validation creates institutional pressure that may cause other forces to deploy unproven models, expanding systemic risk.

themes.theme: UK Government AI Governance and Investment

insights.claim: The UK government launched a new national police AI body, PoliceAI, with £75 million in funding, concurrent with the revelation that existing police AI models had been operating with poor performance for years.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: The simultaneous failure exposure and new £75M investment creates a policy contradiction that could trigger parliamentary scrutiny and tighten AI procurement rules for public-sector buyers—impacting AI vendors seeking government contracts.

insights.claim: This investigation was conducted jointly by WIRED, Liberty Investigates, Bristol Cable, and Lighthouse Reports—a collaborative investigative journalism model that produced evidence strong enough to prompt policy responses.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Multi-outlet investigative coalitions have proven effective at driving AI accountability policy; similar investigations targeting other public-sector AI programs should be expected.

insights.claim: Civil liberties organizations raised concerns that using a database covering 500,000 community members for predictive policing constitutes mass surveillance and carries discriminatory risk given documented biases in similar systems.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Discriminatory bias claims against predictive policing AI create legal liability and reputational risk for both police forces and AI vendors supplying similar tools.

insights.claim: Avon and Somerset Police continued operating predictive AI models for years after receiving internal evidence of poor performance, suggesting institutional inertia prevents AI decommissioning even when models fail validation.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Institutional inertia in AI decommissioning is a systemic risk factor for public-sector AI deployments globally; regulators may respond with mandatory performance thresholds and review cycles.
